CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jun-27-2025 Continued To Sep-24-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-11-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES:

Caleb Marker; 877-500-8780; Zimmerman Reed LLP; counsel for plaintiff appearing via Zoom.
Suzanne Orza; 415-421-6500; Shartsis Friese LLP; counsel for defendant appearing via Zoom.
Reporter: Ryan Wheeler; CSR# 13717; ryanwheeler91@yahoo.com; 760-805-3974

Mr. Marker contests the courts tentative ruling. Argued. After reviewing the pleadings and listening to oral arguments the Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT AIRBNB PAYMENTS, INC., AIRBNB, INC. DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT

is SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND on all causes of action for failure to state a cause of action. While Financial Code section 17003(a) does not contain any reference to an "independent" or "neutral" "third party," the terms of service make clear that Defendant Airbnb Payments Inc. is the disclosed agent of the Host. See Terms of Service 4.1: "Each Host ... hereby appoints Airbnb Payments as the Host's ... agent solely for the limited purpose of accepting and processing funds from Guests...."; 4.3: "Airbnb Payments acts as each Host's payment collection agent for the limited purpose of accepting payments from you on behalf of the Host." Plaintiff has not explained how a third-party can be a "third party" and an agent of one of the parties; or how giving money to someone's agent is not the same as giving money directly to that person, i.e., destroying the whole concept of escrow (no third-party). See 4.2 of the Terms of Service: "Each Host ... agrees that payment made by a Guest through Airbnb Payments shall be considered the same as a payment made directly to the Host...."
As an additional matter, although not argued by Defendants, this Court is not convinced that Plaintiff has standing to bring a UCL claim or that the Financial Code contemplates a private right of action. "To satisfy the narrower standing requirements imposed by Proposition 64, a party must now (1) establish a loss or deprivation of money or property sufficient to qualify as injury in fact, i.e., economic injury, and (2) show that that economic injury was the result of, i.e., caused by, the unfair business practice or false advertising that is the gravamen of the claim." (Kwikset Corp. v. Superior Court (2011) 51 Cal.4th 310, 320, 322.) Plaintiff does not allege that they suffered any particular harm because of Defendant's unlicensed status or because of Defendant's retention of interest it received on funds deposited. Plaintiff does not allege that they relied on Defendant's status as an escrow agent when booking the reservation, or that they paid more for their reservation, or that they were expecting interest to be paid to them, or that they would/could have gone to a different platform to book a reservation had they known of Defendant's unlicensed status. In fact, there are no allegations suggesting that Plaintiff had any belief at all regarding Defendant's status as an escrow agent or not, at the time they booked the reservation. Rather, Plaintiff received the benefit of their bargain, having obtained the bargained for reservation at the bargained for price. (See, Peterson v. Cellco Partnership (2008) 164 Cal.App.4th 1583, 1591.) Plaintiff's one sentence conclusion that Plaintiff "was injured in fact and suffered out-of pocket loss by way of Defendants' conduct ...." (para. 22; see also para. 24,) is not enough. With respect to Plaintiff's allegation that Defendant failed to abide by the escrow instructions, in violation of Fin. Code section 17414, (para. 184(k)), there are no facts supporting this conclusion, namely, identifying the "escrow instructions" provided by Plaintiff and the Host and given to Defendant, and the breach.
Financial Code, section 17701(a) specifically provides for enforcement through action by the commissioner for violations under "Division 6. Escrow Agent." (continued)

Defendants Airbnb, Inc. And Airbnb Payments, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Jul-21-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES:
Flinn Milligan 800-500-8780 of Zimmerman Reed LLP appearing via Zoom for plaintif.
Kasja Minor 415-773-7381 of Shartsis Friese for defendant.
Reporter: Tamara Willat; CSR# 4609; 415-272-1753; tamarawillat@sbcglobal.net

Hearing held (See reporter's transcript for full details). The court calls line 4(CGC-23-604656) and line 6(CGC-23-604821). Argued. After reviewing the pleadings and listening to oral arguments adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT AIRBNB PAYMENTS, INC., AIRBNB, INC. Defendants Airbnb, Inc. And Airbnb Payments, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings

is GRANTED. Parties are bound by the terms of the arbitration agreements and exceptions thereto. There is no dispute as to the existence of the agreements and the language of the relevant provisions, e.g. Terms of Service, sections 23.4 and 23.5. A claim for public injunctive relief along with claims for infringement, emergency injunctive relief, individual claim of sexual assault or harassment is expressly excepted from the arbitration clause and are to be brought in a judicial proceeding in a court of competent jurisdiction. Plaintiff filed this action purportedly asserting a public injunctive relief claim. Defendant appears to argue that before Plaintiff may bring such a claim in Court, Plaintiff must first obtain a ruling from an arbitrator that the claim in fact is one for public injunctive relief: "Taken together, these Sections clearly and unmistakably require Plaintiff to file his claims with the AAA, wherein an arbitrator will decide the arbitrability of Plaintiff's claims, including his or her power to hear those claims under the TOS, the AAA Rules, and the Federal Arbitration Act." (Reply 4:3-6). In other words, defendant appears to argue that before the parties may avail themselves of a judicial proceeding, they need to seek a determination from the arbitrator if their proposed claim in fact is an, for example, "emergency" with "exigent circumstances," or that claim for sexual assault is sufficiently stated, or all elements of copy right infringement claim are asserted and can be proven, or as in this case, a proper claim for public injunction is stated. Defendant cites no authority for this proposition that the claims expressly excepted from the arbitration clause (including emergency relief) first need to be approved by the arbitrator as claims not subjected to the arbitration clause.
Therefore, it is up to this Court to determine whether the claim, as presented to this Court, in the First Amended Complaint falls within the exception to the arbitration clause. Reading the operative complaint as a whole, including specifically paragraphs 92, 93, 99 and 105 the Court finds that this action seeks to redress or prevent injury to an individual plaintiff- or to a group of individuals similarly situated to the plaintiff, that is not an appropriate claim for a public injunctive relief which "by and large benefit[] the general public and - the plaintiff, if at all, only incidentally and/or as a member of the general public."
Judge: Charles F. Haines ; Clerk: L. Young ; Reported: Tamara Willat; CSR# 4609; 415-272-1753; tamarawillat@sbcglobal.net (D501)
